content:
# Final Review Proposal
**Producer:** agent:claude/claude-opus-4-7-max

## Summary

Reviewed the iterative_minimal workflow implementation. The loop termination logic (stable_count vs max_rounds), iteration counter, and resume/recovery paths are correct and well-tested. Five real findings: (high) workspace fingerprint reads unbounded files into memory via fs::read, creating a real OOM risk during iterations; (medium) workspace-level `rb config set` is now blocked by any single corrupt project.toml because the new iterative_minimal validator walks every project; (medium) the `implementer_loop_exited { reason: "error" }` event is emitted when an iteration fails but the loop isn't actually exited — resume re-enters and writes more iteration events, contradicting the event semantics; (low) `git_command_stdout` hardcodes StageId::PlanAndImplement in error output, a latent miscategorization if reused; (low) loop-invariant validation is duplicated in engine.rs and config.rs and will drift.

## Amendments

1. Workspace fingerprint path reads entire files into memory via `fs::read` at `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:6687` (inside `hash_workspace_entries`) and `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:6812` (inside `hash_untracked_git_entry`). For workspaces containing large binaries (build artifacts that aren't excluded, database dumps, media assets, etc.), this will load the full file into RAM just to feed SHA-256 — a multi-GB file would OOM the process. Both paths are exercised on every iterative_minimal iteration: the fallback workspace walker runs twice per iteration when git is unavailable, and `hash_untracked_git_entry` hashes every untracked file even when git is available. Replace `hasher.update(fs::read(&path)?)` with a bounded streaming hash (e.g., `std::io::copy(&mut File::open(&path)?, &mut HashWriter(&mut hasher))` using an 8–64 KiB buffer) so fingerprinting never materializes file contents in memory. Consider also adding a size cap / skip list for large files to bound CPU cost.
   Rationale: Loading unbounded file contents into RAM for hashing is an operational footgun that only manifests under the exact conditions iterative_minimal is meant to support (many iterations against a real workspace). A single large untracked binary can kill the run.
2. `validate_workspace_iterative_minimal_settings` (src/contexts/workspace_governance/config.rs:2305-2317) is invoked from `EffectiveConfig::set_workspace_value` on every `rb config set` at workspace scope (src/contexts/workspace_governance/config.rs:570-572). It enumerates every project directory via `candidate_project_ids_for_iterative_validation` and calls `FileSystem::read_project_config(base_dir, &project_id)?` for each. If any single project has a malformed `project.toml`, the `?` propagates the parse error and blocks *all* workspace-level config edits — including edits to keys unrelated to `workflow.iterative_minimal.*`. Previously a corrupt per-project config only affected that project. Two real issues: (1) a regression in blast radius (unrelated config keys can no longer be set from the workspace level); (2) a latent DoS where accidentally committing a malformed project.toml freezes the whole workspace's config surface. Fix by either (a) only running the cross-project validation when the key being set is one of the `workflow.iterative_minimal.*` keys, or (b) treating per-project read/parse failures as warnings and continuing validation against the remaining projects.
   Rationale: This is a behavior regression caused by the new validation — users setting unrelated workspace config keys shouldn't be blocked by corruption in a single project. Easy to miss in tests because the new tests only cover the happy path for iterative_minimal keys.
3. When an iterative iteration's backend invocation fails, `execute_iterative_plan_and_implement_stage` (src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:7080-7104) appends an `implementer_loop_exited` event with `reason: "error"` and then returns the error. However, the string "error" is not a member of the `IterativeLoopExitReason` enum (src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs: `Stable`, `MaxRounds`). On a subsequent resume, `iterative_loop_exit_recorded` only matches `Stable` / `MaxRounds` reasons, so it does NOT block re-entry; the loop correctly continues iterating. The result is a journal where an `implementer_loop_exited` event is followed by more `implementer_iteration_started` events for the same cursor — contradicting the "loop exited" semantics that external consumers (status/tail UX, observability, the run summary in src/cli/run.rs) rely on. This also means there is no equivalent to "loop_exited stable/max_rounds" idempotency for the error case: if the same error recurs on resume, a second error-event is appended. Fix options: (a) use a new event type like `implementer_iteration_failed` instead of overloading `implementer_loop_exited`; (b) extend `IterativeLoopExitReason` with an `Error` variant and document that "error" exits may be followed by subsequent iterations on resume; or (c) skip the event entirely and rely on `stage_failed` — the error handling around it is already the common path.
   Rationale: The acceptance criteria call for `ImplementerLoopExited { reason: "stable" | "max_rounds" | "error" }`, but the implementation's use of "error" is inconsistent with actual loop termination, creating a contradictory journal. This affects `run tail` output and any downstream analytics.
4. `git_command_stdout` at src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:6763-6768 hardcodes `stage_id: StageId::PlanAndImplement` in the `AppError::StageCommitFailed` error it produces when a git diff command fails. Currently this helper is only called from the iterative plan_and_implement loop, so the error is correct today. But the function is a generic git wrapper named `git_command_stdout` (not `plan_and_implement_git_command_stdout`), and a future caller from any other stage would silently misattribute git failures to `PlanAndImplement`, which then flows into `fail_run` / `failure_label` / status output. Either (a) rename the helper to make the invariant explicit and/or add a debug-assert that asserts the calling stage, or (b) thread the actual `stage_id` through as a parameter. Also relevant: the error classification `StageCommitFailed` is not quite right — no commit is being attempted; this is fingerprint-read failure. Consider a narrower `IterativeDiffFingerprintFailed` variant to avoid polluting `StageCommitFailed` diagnostics.
   Rationale: Low blast radius today but an easy footgun: a maintainer factoring out the git helpers would break error attribution without any failing test.
5. Loop-invariant validation is duplicated between the config layer and the engine layer. `validate_iterative_minimal_loop_settings` at src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:6117-6139 re-implements `validate_effective_iterative_minimal_policy` at src/contexts/workspace_governance/config.rs (see `validate_iterative_minimal_range`). Both check the same invariants (positive values, stable ≤ max) but produce subtly different error messages/types (engine wraps in a runtime `AppError::InvalidConfigValue` while config.rs runs at load time). Maintainers can easily update one without the other, producing a silent skew in error reporting. Since `EffectiveConfig::load` is the only sanctioned way to produce an `EffectiveIterativeMinimalPolicy` and it already enforces the invariants, the engine-side validation is dead defensive code. Remove `validate_iterative_minimal_loop_settings` from engine.rs, or have it delegate to the config-layer validator.
   Rationale: Duplicate validation is a maintainability trap — not a bug today, but invariants will drift. Easy fix during this bead; harder to clean up later once tests are locked in.
